one_line_takeaway: LLM-driven sentiment rewriting is a potent, facts-preserving evasion attack on fake-news detectors -- especially neutralization, which detectors read as 'real' -- and training on sentiment-neutralized data (AdSent) restores robustness and transfers to unseen datasets and style attacks.
key_finding: All evaluated detectors are vulnerable to LLM-driven sentiment manipulation, with macro-F1 dropping by up to 21.51% (RoBERTa on PolitiFact). Counter to prior belief, NEUTRAL sentiment is the most effective evasion: on the neutral variant LLaMA-3.1 shows the lowest F1 and the most fake->real flips (FF->R = 10 on PolitiFact, 128 on GossipCop), because models equate neutral tone with legitimacy. AdSent, trained on neutralized data, achieves the best macro-F1 on the neutralized test sets (87.76 PolitiFact, 78.33 GossipCop per Table 4) and generalizes: it beats SheepDog on all four style-transfer adversarial variants (Table 5) and cross-dataset on LUN (75.13 vs 66.34 F1 original; 70.32 vs 59.67 neutralized), suggesting sentiment shifts perturb predictions more than stylistic changes.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (AdSent) is threefold. (1) A sentiment-targeted adversarial attack: the authors use LLMs as a 'counterfeiter' to rewrite news articles into positive, negative, or neutral sentiment variants while preserving factual content, then measure how much state-of-the-art fake-news detectors degrade. (2) An empirical finding that detectors carry a strong bias to label neutral-toned news as real and non-neutral (emotional) news as fake -- overturning the paper's own initial hypothesis (borrowed from cited work [41]) that positive=real / negative=fake. (3) AdSent itself: a sentiment-agnostic defense that first neutralizes every article's sentiment with the LLM counterfeiter, then fine-tunes an LLM (LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct) detector on those neutralized variants so veracity prediction relies on factual content rather than emotional tone. Note that SheepDog (Wu et al. [37]) is a CITED baseline, not this paper's contribution -- it targets writing-STYLE attacks; AdSent's novelty is targeting SENTIMENT and defending against it.
threat_model: A malicious content creator (adversary) who wants fake news to evade automated detectors. The adversary has black-box access to the detector and uses an LLM to rewrite an article's overall sentiment (positive/negative/neutral) while keeping the underlying facts intact, thereby flipping the detector's veracity prediction. In the paper's threat construction, real news is shifted to negative and fake news to positive to build a 'harder' adversarial test set; a deeper analysis then shows neutralization is actually the most effective evasion (fake-to-real flips). This is an integrity/evasion attack on the detection model, not a data-poisoning or model-extraction attack.
limitations: Text-only, English-only; multimodal misinformation is left to future work. LLM-manipulation experiments are single-run (not averaged over 10 like the others) due to cost, weakening statistical strength of the attack-side numbers. Fact-preservation is verified on only a small human subset (30 PolitiFact articles, 10 per polarity); negative-sentiment rewriting had the lowest fact-preservation (70%) because quotes get altered, so the 'facts preserved' assumption is imperfect. LLM-as-a-judge agreement is only moderate (Cohen's Kappa 0.66). AdSent depends on the same LLM counterfeiter at both train and inference time, adding latency/cost and a potential single point of failure. A minor internal inconsistency exists: prose cites AdSent GossipCop F1 as 78.56 while Table 4 reports 78.33. Only two defense-side datasets (PolitiFact, GossipCop) for the main comparison; broader domains untested beyond the LUN transfer study.

datasets_used: PolitiFact (FakeNewsNet [28]) -- 225 real / 225 fake, temporal 80/20 split (180/180 train, 45/45 test), political content
GossipCop (FakeNewsNet [28]) -- 3,958 real / 3,958 fake (3,166/3,166 train, 792/792 test), celebrity gossip
LUN (Labeled Unreliable News [23]) -- 3,750 real / 3,750 fake (3,000/3,000 train, 750/750 test), satire+hoax+propaganda all treated as fake, used for cross-dataset generalization
quantitative_results: Sentiment attack macro-F1 drop (Table 2): RoBERTa PolitiFact 81.33->59.82 (-21.51), GossipCop 74.05->57.96 (-16.09); SheepDog PolitiFact 89.92->78.44 (-11.48), GossipCop 77.08->70.64 (-6.44); LLaMA-3.1 PolitiFact 72.66->67.74 (-4.92), GossipCop 54.38->50.41 (-3.97); Qwen2.5 PolitiFact 58.40->53.25 (-5.15), GossipCop 46.82->41.35 (-5.47)

Sentiment-variant F1 for LLaMA-3.1 (Table 3): PolitiFact Original 72.66 / Positive 64.16 / Negative 68.87 / Neutral 59.33 (lowest); GossipCop Original 54.38 / Positive 52.29 / Negative 54.29 / Neutral 48.70 (lowest)
Fake->real flips highest under neutral (Table 3): PolitiFact FF->R = 10 (neutral) vs 4 (pos), 2 (neg); GossipCop FF->R = 128 (neutral) vs 26 (pos), 34 (neg)

AdSent on neutralized test sets (Table 4): PolitiFact Acc 87.77, macro-F1 87.76 (best); GossipCop Acc 79.41, macro-F1 78.33 (best) -- vs SheepDog PolitiFact F1 82.98, GossipCop F1 39.63; DeBERTa PolitiFact 67.03, GossipCop 70.35; LLaMA-3.1 PolitiFact 59.33, GossipCop 48.70; Qwen2.5 PolitiFact 52.50, GossipCop 37.05
Style-transfer generalization (Table 5, macro-F1): AdSent beats SheepDog on all variants -- Adv-style-A 85.47 vs 80.99, B 84.38 vs 79.89, C 87.74 vs 82.36, D 86.64 vs 81.24; Neutral 87.76 vs 82.98
Cross-dataset LUN (Table 6, macro-F1): AdSent Original 75.13 vs SheepDog 66.34; Neutral 70.32 vs 59.67

Fact preservation (human, Figure 3): 70%-100% preserved; neutral variant highest at 100% (no fact perturbation), negative lowest; LLM-as-a-judge Cohen's Kappa = 0.66; second-level neutralization F1 within +/-2% of original neutral, max flip deviation 2.33%
baselines_compared: SheepDog (G2: fine-tuned adversarial, style-robust) -- Wu et al. [37]
LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct (G3: zero-shot LLM; also AdSent's backbone) [33]
